content.text: Real-time voice agents with WebRTC + Claude usually use a
hybrid architecture

: WebRTC handles low-latency bidirectional audio transport, while a backend “bot runner” or orchestration service manages VAD, STT, Claude reasoning, TTS, session state, and tool execution. In practice, the most reliable production pattern is not “browser talks directly to Claude,” but “browser joins a real-time media session, backend agent services process the audio stream, and Claude sits inside the server-side reasoning loop.”[][][]
Core architecture

WebRTC’s job is transport: getting microphone audio from user to infrastructure and synthesized audio back with low latency, jitter handling, and NAT traversal. Claude’s job is the language/reasoning layer inside a larger real-time voice pipeline, which still typically includes VAD, STT, orchestration, and TTS rather than raw speech-to-speech Claude transport.[][][]
A canonical production architecture looks like this:
Client
: browser/mobile app captures mic and plays remote audio over WebRTC.[]
Signaling/auth backend
: issues short-lived session or room tokens and sets up the call securely.[]
Media layer
: WebRTC SFU/media server such as LiveKit or Daily-style infra handles audio routing and connection complexity.[]
Bot runner / agent worker
: subscribes to the audio stream, runs VAD + STT + Claude + TTS, publishes audio back.[][][]
App backend
: stores transcripts, tools, analytics, user state, and post-call artifacts.[]
That separation is what makes the system scalable and maintainable.
Why WebRTC

WebRTC is the preferred transport because real-time voice agents are extremely sensitive to latency, packet loss, and device/network variability. WebRTC gives you built-in real-time media transport, congestion control, echo cancellation support paths, NAT traversal, and bidirectional streaming semantics that are much better suited to live conversation than ordinary HTTP upload/download flows.[]
In plain language, WebRTC is the “live phone line,” while Claude is only one participant in the conversation engine behind it.
Main architecture patterns
1. Bot-runner pattern
This is the most common pattern described in real-time voice AI discussions.[]
User joins a WebRTC room/session.
A backend bot/agent also joins the room as a participant.
The bot consumes user audio, runs the AI pipeline, and publishes synthesized speech back into the room.
Why it works:
Clean separation between media transport and AI orchestration.
Easy to add recording, observers, or human handoff.
Natural fit for multi-party rooms or voice agents embedded in meetings.[][]
2. Hybrid WebRTC + WebSocket pattern
A widely used real-world design is:
WebRTC
for audio.
WebSocket
for text events, transcript updates, tool status, UI state, and control messages.[]
This pattern appears repeatedly because audio and app control have different needs:
audio wants low-latency media transport,
app state wants structured event messaging.
Typical split:
WebRTC: mic in, TTS audio out.
WebSocket: partial transcripts, agent state, “thinking,” tool calls, interruptions, session metadata.[]
3. Media-cloud + backend workers
Another common production pattern is:
use managed media infra such as LiveKit-style platforms for room/token handling,
keep Claude, STT, and TTS in your own backend workers.[]

This is popular because teams do not want to debug TURN/STUN and audio routing at scale. The backend still owns identity, session policy, storage, and orchestration, but outsources the hardest low-level media infrastructure.
Request and media flow
A strong real-time flow usually looks like this:
Client asks backend for a short-lived session or room token.[]
Backend authenticates user and mints token from the media provider.[]
Client joins a WebRTC room and starts sending microphone audio.[]
Bot runner receives audio frames or subscribed mixed stream.
VAD detects speech start/end and manages turns.[]
STT transcribes streaming speech.
Claude receives normalized text plus session context and responds.
TTS converts response text into audio.
Agent publishes audio back over WebRTC to the client.[]
This is the real-time equivalent of the classic STT → Claude → TTS cascade, but with WebRTC as the live transport fabric.
Core processing components
Voice Activity Detection

VAD is a foundational component in real-time voice systems because it decides when the user has started or stopped speaking. Without VAD, the system does not know when to transcribe, when to send to Claude, or when to interrupt TTS.[]
Good VAD design enables:
responsive turn-taking,
reduced unnecessary STT load,
barge-in handling,
cleaner latency budgets.
STT layer

Most WebRTC + Claude systems still use a separate streaming STT layer such as Deepgram, Whisper-based services, or another speech recognizer. Whisper is useful for quality and control, but low-latency streaming-first engines are often preferred when real-time interaction is the top priority.[]
Claude reasoning layer

Claude should receive clean, normalized text turns with dialogue context, tool state, and session memory. It is usually not the media-facing component; the orchestration worker or bot runner handles that boundary and calls Claude as the language engine.[][]
TTS layer

TTS usually streams short chunks or sentence-level segments back to audio output. Low-latency voice-agent guides repeatedly emphasize incremental synthesis and streaming audio playback for good perceived responsiveness.[]
Latency architecture

Real-time voice agent UX depends on minimizing end-to-end latency across multiple stages. Voice AI discussions around WebRTC repeatedly stress colocating components, using real-time-optimized models, and reducing delay at every handoff.[][]
Typical latency-sensitive stages are:
audio capture,
network uplink,
VAD decision,
STT first partial transcript,
Claude first token,
TTS first audio byte,
audio playout.
The main lesson is that no single stage can be “pretty good” if you want natural conversation. A mediocre 300 ms in four stages becomes a sluggish experience.
Interruption and barge-in

A real-time voice agent must support interruption. When the user starts speaking while the assistant is still talking, the system should detect new speech, stop or fade TTS playback, and switch back into listening/transcription mode.[]
This is one of the biggest differences between a demo and a production-grade architecture. Good barge-in requires coordination across:
VAD,
playback controller,
transcript state,
active Claude run,
TTS cancellation.
In practical terms, the bot runner needs cancellable pipelines and a session state machine, not just chained API calls.
Session and state management
Real-time voice apps need more session state than text chat because conversation timing matters. A strong backend usually tracks:
session ID / room ID,
participant identity,
recent transcript history,
active speaking side,
whether TTS is playing,
whether Claude is currently generating,
interruption markers,
tool execution state.[]
This is why most serious systems separate the media layer from the application/session backend: the media server moves packets, but your backend owns conversational meaning.
Security pattern
The standard security pattern is:
never expose provider keys directly to browser clients,
keep API credentials on the backend,
mint short-lived access/session tokens for joining media rooms.[]

This is especially important in WebRTC systems because the browser needs enough privilege to join the media session, but not enough privilege to access your AI providers, tool backends, or tenant-wide resources.
A secure architecture typically includes:
user auth with your own backend,
backend-issued room token,
server-side Claude/STT/TTS credentials,
tenant-aware policy in the bot runner.
Deployment patterns
Single-region bot workers
Simplest architecture:
one region,
media provider,
one backend cluster for agent workers.
Good for prototypes and moderate load.
Regional edge media + centralized AI
WebRTC/media edge close to users,
Claude/STT/TTS workers centralized.
Easier operations, but some latency penalty between media edge and AI stack.[]
Distributed workers
regional media entry,
regional bot workers near users,
shared session/control plane.
Best for premium low-latency experiences, but most complex.
Good production choices
Several practical choices show up again and again in modern real-time voice agent stacks:
Use managed WebRTC/SFU infrastructure unless media networking is your specialty.[]
Keep credentials server-side and mint short-lived tokens for clients.[]
Separate audio transport from app control, often with WebRTC + WebSocket together.[]
Treat the bot as a participant in the media session rather than bolting AI onto the side.[]
Design around VAD, interruption, and latency budgets from day one.[][]
Persist transcripts and session events in the app backend for analytics and recovery.[]
Common failure modes
The most common architectural mistakes are:
Putting provider secrets in the client.
Using plain HTTP uploads instead of real-time media transport for live voice.
Ignoring interruption/barge-in until late in the build.[]
Treating transcripts as stateless when the agent actually needs a session model.
Coupling media transport and Claude orchestration too tightly.
Relying on in-process state only, which breaks reconnects and scaling.
Underestimating TURN/NAT/media complexity, which is why managed platforms are so common.[]
Layman’s explanation

Think of WebRTC as the live audio cable and Claude as the brain attached to a robot operator. The cable carries sound both ways very quickly, but the robot operator still needs ears, a brain, a voice, and a memory. In a real system, WebRTC is not the whole agent—it is the fast pipe that lets the whole voice stack behave like a real conversation.
Study questions with answers
Why use WebRTC for Claude voice agents?
Because WebRTC is built for low-latency bidirectional real-time media transport, which voice agents need.[]
What is the bot-runner pattern?
It is an architecture where an AI worker joins the WebRTC session as a participant, consumes audio, runs STT → Claude → TTS, and publishes audio back.[]
Why combine WebRTC and WebSocket?
Because WebRTC is ideal for audio, while WebSocket is often simpler for transcripts, control events, and tool/status messages.[]
Why not connect the browser directly to Claude?
Because real-time voice still needs orchestration, STT/TTS, auth, session control, and tool execution on the backend.[][]
What makes a WebRTC voice architecture production-grade?
Secure token minting, media/session separation, VAD, interruption handling, scalable bot workers, and latency-aware orchestration.[]
